stories.title: New Houston Rental Community Rents Start at $9,480
stories.excerpt: The Langley, a 20-story residential high-rise developed by StreetLights Residential and Hunt Real Estate, has completed construction at the intersection of Houston’s historic Boulevard Oaks and Southampton neighborhoods. Located at 1717 Bissonnet Street near Rice University, The Langley features 134 luxury residences for lease. Two- and three-bedroom homes range from approximately 2,165 to 3,396 square feet, with monthly rents starting at approximately $9,480, with some rents reaching up to $20,000 a month. With just eight residences per floor, the tower departs from traditional high-rise apartment layouts in favor of a more intimate and private residential experience. The development’s signature outdoor amenities include a pool deck, cabanas, grilling areas, landscaped paseos, and a commercial-grade dual-fuel pizza oven imported and installed as a centerpiece of the outdoor entertaining experience. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post New Houston Rental Community Rents Start at $9,480 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: StreetLights Residential and Hunt completed 134-unit luxury high-rise at 1717 Bissonnet, Houston near Rice University
Market rents $9,480–$20,000/month for 2–3 bed units (2,165–3,396 sf); only 8 units per floor
Ultra-premium positioning signals strong demand and pricing power in Boulevard Oaks/Southampton submarket
stories.summary: The Langley, a 20-story luxury development by StreetLights and Hunt, delivered 134 units near Rice University in Houston's Boulevard Oaks neighborhood. Rents range from $9,480 to $20,000/month for two- and three-bedroom units, with intimate layouts (8 units/floor) and high-end amenities. The project reflects confident positioning in a high-barrier-to-entry submarket.
stories.impact: Ultra-luxury rents at $9,480+ in Boulevard Oaks signal sustained pricing power and demand from affluent tenants near Rice. Developers should note the receptiveness of this affluent Houston submarket to premium-priced, limited-supply product—a potential template for similar mixed-income/career-hub markets seeking to drive rent growth above metro averages.

stories.title: Rise48 Equity Acquires N. Phoenix Rental Community
stories.excerpt: Tailwind Investment Group offloaded Rise at the Northern, a 108-unit apartment community at 17840. N. Black Canyon Highway in far north Phoenix. Rise48 Equity paid $21.7 million for the multifamily venture. Rise48 Equity pre-empted the offer process and submitted a full-price offer with a non-refundable deposit on Day 1. Northmarq’s Phoenix Investment Sales (IS) team of Jesse Hudson, Chris Michl and Logan Baca arranged the sale for Tailwind. The property, formerly known as The Northern, was built in 1983. Situated on 3.62 acres within the Deer Valley submarket Rise at the Northern consists of four three-story residential buildings with a mix of one- and two-bedroom floor plans averaging 868 square feet. At the time of sale, approximately 20% of units had been renovated. Rise48 Equity plans a light value-add renovation across 100% of the units. Community amenities include a swimming pool with lounge chairs and shade structures, heated jacuzzi, outdoor dining area and ramada, fitness center, and picnic area and BBQ grills. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Rise48 Equity Acquires N. Phoenix Rental Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Rise48 Equity acquired 108-unit Rise at the Northern, 1983-built, Deer Valley Phoenix for $21.7M
Only 20% of units renovated at sale; buyer plans full light value-add across 100% of portfolio
Day-1 preemptive bid on <$200K/unit asset signals investor confidence in Phoenix value-add market
stories.summary: Rise48 Equity acquired the 108-unit Rise at the Northern in Phoenix's Deer Valley submarket for $21.7 million ($201K/unit). The 41-year-old property was only 20% renovated at sale. Rise48 plans a comprehensive light value-add across all units, having preempted the formal marketing process with a full-price, non-refundable Day 1 offer.
stories.impact: Aggressive acquisition and immediate value-add pipeline in Deer Valley signals robust investor appetite for class-B, older Phoenix multifamily. Thin cap rates and strong demand for repositionable inventory compress pricing and entry windows for development-stage competitors; near-market delivery of 100% renovated units may create rental comp pressure in North Phoenix if absorption lags.

stories.title: SDC Capital Partners Inks 23K-SF Lease at Savanna’s 799 Broadway
stories.excerpt: SDC Capital Partners has signed a new office lease just south of Manhattan’s Union Square, Commercial Observer has learned. The global digital infrastructure investment firm is taking 22,531 square feet of office space at Savanna’s 799 Broadway, according to a source with knowledge of the deal. The lease seems to represent a minor relocation for SDC, which lists its current offices a block north at 817 Broadway. For its new office, the firm signed a 12-year lease on the entire 12th floor of 799 Broadway, bringing the property on the corner of East 11th Street to 100 percent occupancy, Savanna told CO. Savanna did not disclose the name of the building’s new tenant, but a source confirmed it was SDC. SDC did not immediately respond to CO’s request for comment. The asking rent for the new lease was not disclosed. The average asking rent for office space in the Union Square/Flatiron District area was $86.96 per square foot during the second quarter, according to data from Newmark. The tenant was represented by Cushman & Wakefield‘s Evan Algier, David Mainthow, Michael Montesi and Nick Masi. Savanna was represented by JLL‘s Benjamin Bass, Mitchell Konsker and Thomas Swartz. Both C&W and JLL declined to comment. “Achieving full office occupancy at 799 Broadway is a tremendous accomplishment for both Savanna and our partner, Anastacia, and a testament to the quality of the asset, its exceptional location and the strength of demand for best-in-class office space,” Brian Reiver, chief real estate officer at Savanna, said in a statement. “When we acquired the property together, we recognized the opportunity to build upon an already strong foundation and create one of Lower Manhattan’s premier office destinations.” Built in 2022, 799 Broadway is a 12-story office building with tenants including venture capital firm Lightspeed and investment firm Wellington Management. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: SDC Capital Partners signed 22,531-sf office lease at Savanna's 799 Broadway, Union Square NYC
12-year lease on full 12th floor; property now 100% occupied; tenant relocated <1 block north
Market rents ~$87/sf; minimal movement signals strong coworking/flex demand in Union Square submarket
stories.summary: Digital infrastructure firm SDC Capital Partners leased 22,531 sf at 799 Broadway in Manhattan's Union Square/Flatiron district on a 12-year lease. The deal brought the property to full occupancy. SDC relocated from a building one block north, suggesting minimal tenant churn and strong locational stickiness despite market softness.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Office absorption in Union Square does not directly signal residential demand; however, persistent office-to-residential conversion interest in Midtown East and Manhattan at large reflects longer-term office distress that may accelerate adaptive-reuse opportunities and debt/equity availability for conversion players.

stories.title: Feil Organization Hires Samantha Perlman to Help Lead Commercial Leasing
stories.excerpt: The Feil Organization has hired Samantha Perlman as its new associate director of commercial leasing. Perlman, who most recently served as a senior associate at Cushman & Wakefield, will help lead leasing efforts for Feil’s New York City office portfolio, which spans approximately 7.2 million square feet across about 42 properties, according to the management and development firm. “The Feil Organization has built an exceptional portfolio backed by long-term ownership, a hands-on approach and an unwavering commitment to its tenants,” Perlman said in a statement. “I’m excited to join the team and build on the firm’s success while helping deliver the personalized service and dedicated tenant relationships that have defined the Feil Organization for generations.” During her time at C&W, Perlman had a hand in leasing at 18 Manhattan properties totaling 5 million square feet while managing transactions through lease execution, preparing proposals, and negotiating lease terms, according to Feil. Prior to her role at C&W, Perlman graduated from the University of Michigan’s Ross School of Business and held intern roles at Jafri Capital, Tamarack Real Estate Services and Turnberry, according to her LinkedIn profile. It’s unclear when Perlman will start in her new position at Feil. “Samantha brings a thoughtful, tenant-focused approach, and her background makes her well positioned to continue the strong leasing momentum in our New York City office portfolio,” Andrew Wiener, head of commercial leasing at Feil, said in a statement. “As we continue investing in our properties and strengthening relationships with our tenants, Samantha’s expertise will be a tremendous asset to our team and our continued growth.” Perlman’s hire comes after some big leasing news at Feil’s Manhattan office buildings recently. In May, engineering firm HDR signed a deal for 74,500 square feet at the firm’s 7 Penn Plaza, while poverty-relief nonprofit Robin Hood took 53,000 square feet at Fei
bullets: Feil Organization hired Samantha Perlman as Associate Director of Commercial Leasing from Cushman & Wakefield
Feil portfolio spans 7.2M sf across ~42 Manhattan office properties; C&W leasing experience covers 5M sf/18 properties
Talent consolidation reinforces scale and hands-on management model among largest NYC office holders
stories.summary: The Feil Organization hired Samantha Perlman as Associate Director of Commercial Leasing to manage the company's 7.2 million-sf office portfolio across ~42 Manhattan properties. Perlman previously handled 5 million sf of leasing at Cushman & Wakefield. The move reflects Feil's strategy to centralize leasing talent and strengthen tenant relationships across a vast holdings base.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. Illustrates consolidation and operational sophistication among Manhattan's largest office holders—potential upstream indicator of asset quality and proactive management that may translate to conversion-readiness if/when markets favor office-to-residential transitions.

stories.title: Livmark Lands $115.5M Construction Loan for Ft. Collins Apartments
stories.excerpt: Livmark Properties obtained a $115.5 million construction loan for Collins at Union Park, a 457-unit luxury multifamily community in Fort Collins, Colorado. The debt, arranged by Concord Summit Capital, was structured on a non-recourse basis with an 88% loan-to-cost ratio. Collins at Union Park is a thoughtfully designed residential development featuring upscale apartments and carriage homes. Planned amenities include a clubhouse, pool, golf simulator, playground, parks, trails, and gathering spaces. The project is situated within walking distance of Front Range Village Shopping Center. Site work is underway, and vertical construction is expected to start immediately at the project site located on Un Pk Ave in Fort Collins, CO 80525. The financing was arranged by Concord Summit Capital’s Daniel J. Eidson, Keegan Burger and Ben Applebaum on behalf of Livmark. “We successfully structured one loan facility to combine two phases into a single development, while negotiating a meaningful rate stepdown upon completion,” said Concord’s Eidson. The post Livmark Lands $115.5M Construction Loan for Ft. Collins Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Livmark Properties secured $115.5M construction loan at 88% LTC for Collins at Union Park, Fort Collins (457 units)
Non-recourse structured debt with rate stepdown on completion; vertical construction imminent on 457-unit luxury community
Colorado multifamily financing availability and strong LTC at scale demonstrates lender comfort in Denver-area frontrange markets
stories.summary: Livmark Properties obtained $115.5 million in non-recourse construction financing from Concord Summit Capital for Collins at Union Park, a 457-unit luxury community in Fort Collins, Colorado. The loan carried an 88% LTC and included a meaningful rate stepdown upon project completion. Vertical construction is beginning immediately.
stories.impact: Colorado multifamily lending at 88% LTC and non-recourse terms signals lender confidence in Fort Collins' demand tailwinds and capture of Denver-area population migration. Developers considering Front Range entry should note robust debt availability; however, rising supply (visible in major starts like this) may compress rent growth and cap rates within 24–36 months if demand growth stalls.

stories.title: S3 Capital Lends $111M for Miami Resi Tower
stories.excerpt: Argentine developer HA Emprendimientos has sealed $111 million of construction financing to build a luxury multifamily tower in Miami, Commercial Observer has learned. S3 Capital provided the loan for the 36-story Sense 22 project in Miami’s Edgewater neighborhood. Gonzalo Espinosa, chief financial officer at HA Emprendimientos, acquired the development site for Sense 22 at 114-138 NE 22nd Street for $8 million in December 2023 from Cain. JLL’s Max La Cava negotiated the debt. “We like the Edgewater market because of its premier location,” Steven Jemal, managing director of Origination at S3 Capital, said in a statement. “It’s close enough to Biscayne Boulevard, Wynwood and the Design District to stay connected to Miami’s core, without asking residents to live in the middle of downtown.” Jemal added that “recent absorption trends” for the multifamily sector in Miami and tax advantages under Florida’s Live Local Act “made Sense 22 a compelling opportunity” for S3. Located about two miles north of Downtown Miami, Sense 22 will feature 328 apartments ranging from studios to three-bedroom units, along with 372 parking spaces. Community amenities will include a swimming pool, a rooftop terrace with outdoor grilling areas, a spa, a fitness center and coworking spaces. “We identified Edgewater as a neighborhood with tremendous growth potential and proven financial performance,” Ignacio Aldazabal, CEO of HA Emprendimientos, said in a statement. The project spans approximately 262,000 net rentable square feet, with an average unit size of 799 square feet. South Florida-based Grycon is serving as general contractor for the Sense 22 development, which is slated for completion in late 2028 “Seeing the project take shape is a fulfilment of the developers’ vision for the future of Miami’s quickly evolving skyline,” La Cava, managing director at JLL, said in a statement. “Sense 22 is setting a new standard for urban living and cementing Edgewater as one of Miami’
bullets: S3 Capital provided $111M construction loan for 36-story Sense 22 (328 units) in Miami Edgewater
Land acquired December 2023 for $8M; project targets Edgewater's proximity to Biscayne Boulevard, Wynwood, Design District
Lender cited 'recent absorption trends' and Live Local Act tax benefits as deal drivers in Miami multifamily
stories.summary: S3 Capital closed $111 million in construction financing for Sense 22, a 36-story luxury multifamily tower with 328 units in Miami's Edgewater neighborhood. Developer HA Emprendimientos acquired the land in December 2023 for $8 million. The lender highlighted Miami's strong multifamily absorption and Florida's Live Local Act tax incentives as key underwriting drivers.
stories.impact: Miami Edgewater is attracting capital on strength of absorption momentum and tax incentives under Live Local. Strong financing availability and lender appetite for Edgewater (now a secondary location vs. downtown/Brickell) signals developer confidence in rental demand spillover from downtown saturation. Expect accelerating competitive supply deliveries; developers must secure entitlements and financing early to avoid cap-rate compression downstream.

stories.title: Texas Southern Debuts $1.7B Campus Master Plan
stories.excerpt: Texas Southern University in Houston has launched a Campus Master Plan that establishes a framework for future campus development. The plan outlines an approximately $1.7 billion campus investment and more than 20 phased projects over the next decade. It aligns future capital investments with the University’s strategic priorities. The Master Plan identifies a series of intersecting phased capital projects that support student success, workforce preparation, research expansion, and community engagement. Future priorities include student-centered facilities, academic and research infrastructure, residential and recreational amenities, and spaces that strengthen connections between the University and the broader community. Several major projects are already advancing. Construction on three new academic and research facilities – the interconnected Catalyst for Urban Transformation, the Nabrit Science Center, and the Health and Wellness Center, is expected to begin later this year. Supported by more than $95 million in state funding, all three facilities are expected to open during the University’s Centennial year. The post Texas Southern Debuts $1.7B Campus Master Plan appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Texas Southern University announced $1.7B Campus Master Plan over 10 years; 20+ phased projects in Houston
Three major facilities (Catalyst for Urban Transformation, Nabrit Science Center, Health & Wellness) starting later 2024
$95M state funding committed; facilities expected to open during University's Centennial year
stories.summary: Texas Southern University launched a $1.7 billion Campus Master Plan featuring 20+ phased projects over the next decade in Houston. Construction on three interconnected flagship facilities—Catalyst for Urban Transformation, Nabrit Science Center, and Health & Wellness Center—is slated to begin later in 2024 with $95 million in state funding and completion during the university's centennial year.
stories.impact: Major institutional investment and construction activity at TSU (Third Ward/Midtown Houston) will drive local job creation, student/faculty population retention, and ancillary demand for student and workforce housing. Developers should scout nearby residential land and partner opportunities; TSU's commitment signals confidence in the Third Ward's long-term trajectory and may catalyze broader neighborhood investment and rent growth in adjacent submarkets.

stories.title: Pfizer Building Collapse Stabilized as Metro Loft, David Werner Assess Damage
stories.excerpt: Workers managed to stabilize the former Pfizer headquarters building at 235 East 42nd Street and 219 East 42nd Street overnight after columns buckled under the weight of added floors early Tuesday. The near-disaster put the surrounding blocks of Midtown East on high alert as city authorities evacuated the area and suspended activity in neighboring buildings. Metro Loft’s Nathan Berman has since described the event as a “freak accident.” What the crisis means in the long run for Metro Loft and its development partner David Werner — or for the future of office-to-residential conversions in general — is unclear. But the high-profile nature of the development itself, as the largest ongoing office-to-residential conversion in the U.S. with the addition of 11 new stories, takes the implications beyond just liability and insurance. “This is a freak accident that something occurred with these two specific columns that either were not reinforced or were not reinforced sufficiently, and they gave way,” Berman told The Real Deal Tuesday evening. “It’s very simple. You add more load to something that can’t support it, it’ll give way, and that’s what happened, and now it just needs to be fixed.” But in the past, the building was no stranger to incidents and violations. There were previous complaints filed with the New York City Department of Buildings (DOB) that a worker fell six feet off a ladder, a metal panel fell from the 33rd floor to the street level below, and unapproved excavations took place in the cellar, according to reports. Possibly the most alarming complaint filed with the DOB came from October 2025. “Unlicensed workers and unsafe conditions,” the complaint said. “Today a large item fell and broke through five floors and almost hit someone. There are gas machines without proper ventilation. These are hazardous conditions for the workers. The wielders are unlicensed, there aren’t any spotters, and the sparks hit workers because they don’t use a safety b
bullets: Columns buckled at 235 East 42nd Street (former Pfizer HQ) during night stabilization; MetroLoft/David Werner conversion adding 11 floors
Near-collapse forced street evacuations; largest office-to-residential conversion in U.S. with structural safety now in public eye
Incident raises regulatory scrutiny and insurance/liability implications for similar high-profile conversion projects nationwide
stories.summary: Columns buckled overnight at the former Pfizer headquarters (235 East 42nd Street) during stabilization work. MetroLoft's Nathan Berman downplayed the incident as a "freak accident," attributing it to insufficient reinforcement under added construction loads. The event forced evacuations and street closures in Midtown East and thrust the nation's largest ongoing office-to-residential conversion into a high-profile safety crisis.
stories.impact: Structural failure at Metro Loft's flagship 1,500+ unit conversion—the largest in the U.S.—will accelerate regulatory scrutiny and increase insurance/liability costs for office-to-residential conversions across major markets. NYC DOB may impose stricter load-path and structural reinforcement requirements, raising hard costs and extending timelines for conversion entitlements and financing approvals. Lenders may demand enhanced structural reviews, depressing capital availability and conversion deal flow in the near term.

stories.title: S2 Acquires 589K-SF FW Industrial Campus
stories.excerpt: Lonpoint Realty Partners sold Point 820, a 589,022-square-foot campus in Fort Worth, Texas, to S2 Industrial. S2 will rebrand it as Panther City Industrial Park. Panther City Industrial Park comprises 31 buildings developed between 1966 and 1968 across a 34-acre site. The campus is at 5721 E. Rosedale St., just off Interstate 820, also providing easy access to state routes 303 and 180, as well as interstates 20, 30 and 35. Commercial Search reports that, because the previous ownership has recently renovated the building’s common areas and exterior facades, the new owner plans to invest in upgrading suite interiors. S2 Industrial’s property management arm, Fort Management, will handle day-to-day operations at the property. Dallas-Fort Worth has registered more than $2.3 billion in industrial investment sales year-to-date through May, according to a recent Yardi Matrix report, ranking first in the U.S. Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players.  Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post S2 Acquires 589K-SF FW Industrial Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: S2 Industrial acquired Point 820 (589,022 sf, 31 buildings) in Fort Worth from Lonpoint Realty Partners
Campus rebranded as Panther City Industrial Park; built 1966–1968; off I-820 with easy access to major interstates/state routes
DFW industrial sales exceeded $2.3B YTD through May 2024; new owner planning suite interior upgrades
stories.summary: S2 Industrial acquired Point 820, a 589,022-sf industrial campus comprising 31 buildings developed between 1966 and 1968, from Lonpoint Realty Partners in Fort Worth. S2 rebranded it as Panther City Industrial Park and plans interior suite upgrades. The property sits on 34 acres off I-820 with direct access to major transportation corridors.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. However, strong industrial investment activity in DFW ($2.3B YTD through May) and logistics/distribution employment growth support population and wage growth in Fort Worth, bolstering downstream multifamily demand and rent stability, particularly in logistics-adjacent submarkets.

stories.title: Uptown Theatre owner seeks $200M overhaul with bigger city subsidy
stories.excerpt: How much money is enough to renovate a classic Chicago landmark? The developers aiming to revamp the long-shuttered Uptown Theatre at 4816 North Broadway and the city of Chicago are about to find out.  Uptown Theatre owner Jerry Mickelson said that a full renovation would come with a $200 million price tag, and the majority of that funding would have to be public, according to Block Club Chicago. He went on to cite a downtown office building on LaSalle Street that received $98 million from the city in tax increment financing, while he’s been offered around half of that figure.  […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago Uptown Theatre owner Jerry Mickelson seeks $200M renovation; requesting TIF subsidy >50% of total cost
Previous $79M 2018 deal collapsed when partner Farpoint Development exited in 2021; COVID cited as factor
TIF guidance caps assistance at 30%; Mickelson claims 35% insufficient; comparable Congress Theater project used 31% TIF
stories.summary: Uptown Theatre owner Jerry Mickelson is seeking a $200 million renovation with majority public funding via TIF (Tax Increment Financing). His 2024 application requests assistance well above the city's 30% TIF guidance; a prior $79 million deal collapsed in 2021 when partner Farpoint Development exited, citing pandemic conditions. Comparable Congress Theater used 31% TIF support.
stories.impact: Stalled landmark conversion tied to financing gap signals broader challenge for adaptive-reuse and historic preservation projects in secondary/tertiary markets: modest private equity appetite and tight TIF/PILOT availability limit feasibility studies and kill deals early. Developers eyeing similar cultural/entertainment conversions should expect long entitlement timelines, political friction, and limited public subsidy—raising equity requirements and depressing project returns.

stories.title: Rockpoint and Urby Form JV to Develop Luxury Resi Tower on the Jersey City Waterfront
stories.excerpt: A new, ultra-luxury residential tower with views of the Manhattan skyline is getting ready to rise across the Hudson River. Rockpoint and Urby have formed a joint venture to acquire the land at 201 Hudson Street on the Jersey City waterfront and develop phase two of a three-tower multifamily development on the coveted site, Commercial Observer has learned. Once completed, the new building, 201 Hudson — by Urby, will feature 748 market-rate apartments, 10,000 square feet of retail and 102 parking spots. Designed with commuters to Manhattan and other parts of New Jersey in mind as residents, the new tower will feature top-tier amenities and is expected to take three years to complete. Newmark’s Adam Spies, Adam Doneger and Michael Collins advised both Urby and Rockpoint on the transaction and, along with a Newmark team of Jordy Roeschlaub and Chris Kramer, are currently in the market with the construction financing mandate, seeking $267 million for the project, sources familiar with the offering memorandum told Commercial Observer. Brookfield is the seller of the site, and the partnership between the buyers has been years in the making. Boston-based Rockpoint made a preferred equity investment in Roseland Residential Trust, the multifamily subsidiary of Mack-Cali Realty Corporation — now Veris Residential — in 2018, and got to know the Urby team tangentially through that investment. When Urby developed the first tower, Jersey City Urby — now known as Sable — with Roseland and Ironstate Development, Rockpoint had a firsthand view. “We had a front-row seat watching that tower’s construction, which was really transformational for that submarket at the time,” Dan Domb, a managing member and chief operating officer of Rockpoint, said. Rockpoint exited that investment in 2023. At the time, the development was structured as having two phases, and Newmark’s Spies and Doneger suggested that Rockpoint consider working with Urby on the next tower. “It took a while to come togeth
bullets: Rockpoint and Urby JV to develop 201 Hudson Street, Jersey City waterfront; phase 2 of 3-tower project
Planned: 748 market-rate apartments, 10,000 sf retail, 102 parking; 3-year construction window; seeking $267M construction financing
Manhattan skyline views and NJ Transit access position project as high-demand commuter/remote-work residential
stories.summary: Rockpoint and Urby formed a joint venture to acquire and develop 201 Hudson Street on the Jersey City waterfront as phase two of a three-tower residential complex. The new tower will deliver 748 market-rate apartments, 10,000 sf of retail, and 102 parking spaces over three years. The partnership is currently marketing $267 million in construction financing through Newmark.
stories.impact: Waterfront Jersey City multifamily continues to attract top-tier capital and JV sponsors, signaling sustained demand from Manhattan-commuting and remote workers seeking lower costs and lifestyle amenities. Strong construction financing appetite ($267M mandate) suggests healthy lender appetite for well-located NJ residential; however, rising supply in Jersey City waterfront (Urby 2 plus competing deliveries) may compress rent growth—developers should focus on distinctive amenity/design positioning and early lease-up momentum.

stories.title: “People come here to make babies”: Mapping development in Hallandale Beach
stories.excerpt: Hallandale Beach is emerging as the next Broward County development target with a surge of projects entering the city’s pipeline. Projects underway include residential towers, a branded hotel and an office development.  Hallandale’s appeal is twofold: Rising prices in Miami-Dade and Palm Beach counties are pushing buyers into Broward, and the city’s location between Miami and Fort Lauderdale makes it an attractive base, said Scott Patterson, who leads the Scott Patterson Group and specializes in luxury and waterfront real estate throughout Hallandale Beach and South Florida. “People come here to make babies; they don’t [just] come here to vacation anymore,” […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hallandale Beach emerging as Broward County development hub: rising Miami-Dade/Palm Beach prices pushing buyers into Hallandale
Kadima Developers' Seven Park: $70M, 121 condos, starting at $375K ($750/sf average) targets priced-out luxury buyers
Eight-story mixed-use with 300K sf amenities signals suburban shift and value-driven buyer migration between Miami/Fort Lauderdale
stories.summary: Hallandale Beach is emerging as a high-growth development node between Miami and Fort Lauderdale, driven by price migration from Miami-Dade and Palm Beach counties. Kadima Developers' Seven Park ($70M, 8-story mixed-use) features 121 condos starting at $375K ($750/sf average), targeting buyers priced out of newer luxury but wary of aging stock. Projects underway include additional residential towers, hotel, and office.
stories.impact: Hallandale's position as an affordability valve between Miami and Fort Lauderdale signals structural demand tailwinds for multifamily and for-sale product. Developers should monitor absorption rates and rent/price stickiness; rapid supply additions could compress achievable yields if job and population migration into Broward slows. Early-stage entitlements and land acquisition in Hallandale's secondary corridors may offer value before cap rates compress.

stories.title: “Freak accident”: Pfizer building developer denies risk of collapse
stories.excerpt: The developer behind the conversion of the former Pfizer headquarters in Midtown is speaking out to quell concerns after the massive development site was the subject of an emergency response early Tuesday. Hours after city officials warned of an “extremely dangerous situation” at the in-progress conversion of the former office property, Nathan Berman of MetroLoft said in an interview with The Real Deal that reports of the building’s impending collapse have been “blown a little bit out of proportion,” adding that despite videos of sagging upper floors and buckled support columns, the building “was never at risk of collapse” and […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MetroLoft Nathan Berman denies collapse risk at 235 East 42nd Street (former Pfizer HQ) despite buckled columns, sagging floors
Berman claims issue was added construction weight on two specific under-reinforced columns; disputes insufficient steel design allegations
Developer maintains building 'never at risk of collapse' and issues are 'fixable'; project aims to exceed 1,320-unit 25 Water Street record
stories.summary: MetroLoft's Nathan Berman publicly responded to structural concerns at the former Pfizer headquarters conversion (235 East 42nd Street), attributing the incident to added construction weight exceeding the capacity of two specific columns. Berman disputed claims of inadequate steel reinforcement and maintained the building was never at risk of collapse. If completed, the project would be the largest office-to-residential conversion in U.S. history, surpassing his prior 25 Water Street (1,320 units).
stories.impact: Public reassurance from a prominent conversion developer does little to mitigate regulatory, insurance, and market-perception damage from a high-profile structural failure at the nation's most visible office-to-residential conversion. Lenders and insurers will demand independent third-party reviews and enhanced structural remediation plans across all active conversion pipelines, raising hard costs 5–15% and compressing conversion deal flow until confidence is restored. Conversion cap rates will widen in the near term.

stories.title: Related Group’s Hollywood Live Local project draws suit from residents, nonprofit
stories.excerpt: Hollywood residents are jumping into the brawl over Florida’s developer-friendly Live Local Act with a lawsuit to block a planned beachfront tower.  Kathleen DiBona and the newly formed nonprofit Keep Public Lands Public is suing the City of Hollywood and an affiliate of Related Group over the proposed project at 1301 South Ocean Boulevard, claiming it violates a deed restriction on the site and should go before a public referendum, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  Related Group has a 99-year lease on the 4-acre site fronting a public beach, with plans to build a luxury condo and community center. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Keep Public Lands Public and resident Kathleen DiBona sued City of Hollywood and Related Group affiliate over 1301 South Ocean Boulevard project
Related holds 99-year lease on 4-acre beachfront; Live Local Act allowed bypass of rezoning; project includes 201 residences (84 workforce units)
Lawsuit challenges deed restriction and city charter, requiring voter approval for 50+ year park/recreational property leases east of Intracoastal
stories.summary: Residents and newly formed nonprofit Keep Public Lands Public sued the City of Hollywood and a Related Group affiliate over the planned Live Local Act project at 1301 South Ocean Boulevard. The 4-acre beachfront property is subject to deed restrictions and city charter rules that nominally require voter approval for 50+ year leases of public recreational land. Related negotiated a 99-year lease and planned development of 201 residences (84 workforce units) and a community center via the streamlined Live Local process.
stories.impact: Live Local Act litigation in Hollywood signals political/legal backlash to developer-friendly regulatory streamlining when applied to publicly held or deed-restricted waterfront land. Developers pursuing Live Local projects on public or quasi-public sites should conduct early legal due diligence on deed restrictions, charter provisions, and community opposition risk; legal/entitlement timelines may extend significantly, raising carry costs and deal risk. This may slow Live Local adoption in South Florida and similar markets.

stories.title: Nahla’s $1B Raleigh Miami Beach expansion proposal secures blessing from board despite height concerns
stories.excerpt: Nahla Capital moved one step closer to securing approval for tweaks that will allow the developer to increase the size of the proposed Raleigh Miami Beach, a Rosewood-branded luxury condo and hotel project that’s been in the works for years.  Nahla acquired the 3-acre oceanfront assemblage at 1775, 1757 and 1751 Collins Avenue in October for $270 million, taking over from developer Michael Shvo and his partners.  At a Miami Beach Planning Board meeting on Tuesday, the board voted 5 to 1 to recommend approval of comprehensive plan and code amendments that will allow the developer to increase the floor […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Nahla Capital secured 5–1 planning board vote recommending approval for Raleigh Miami Beach expansion (Rosewood-branded hotel/condo)
Project to increase FAR from 2.0 to 2.5, adding 66,000 sf via 2 residential floors; total project size ~332,000 sf ($1B investment plan)
Nahla is 5th owner in 15 years; hotel closed since 2017; acquisition price $270M for 3-acre oceanfront assemblage (Oct 2023)
stories.summary: Nahla Capital secured a 5–1 planning board vote recommending approval of comprehensive plan amendments to increase the floor area ratio from 2.0 to 2.5 for the Raleigh Miami Beach project. The expansion adds ~66,000 sf via two additional residential floors. Nahla acquired the 3-acre oceanfront assemblage for $270 million in October 2023. Total project investment is projected at ~$1 billion.
stories.impact: Successful planning approval and Nahla's large-scale capital commitment ($1B+) signal strong institutional appetite for luxury hotel/condo mixed-use on Miami Beach. However, Raleigh's history (5 owners in 15 years, closed since 2017) reflects long-cycle entitlements and capital intensity for ultra-luxury hospitality-residential hybrids. Developers should note that even well-capitalized sponsors face execution and absorption risk in luxury hotel/condo products; focus on early/strong leasing commitments and diversified revenue streams to de-risk long hold periods.

stories.title: Meet the developers behind the Pfizer building conversion
stories.excerpt: New York City turned its eyes towards the former Pfizer building in Midtown East Tuesday morning, but not for the reason the developers would’ve hoped. Reports emerged before 8 a.m. of debris falling from the construction site at 235 East 42nd Street, arguably the country’s most notable office-to-residential conversion post-pandemic to unfold. As of midday, concerns about the safety of the building remain as columns within the building buckle, forcing evacuations and street closures in and around the property. It’s not the first notable incident at the project, which commanded real estate’s attention long before pedestrians were forced to steer […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
